coredata.dc:title: Bifunctional gelatin/dextrin hybrid backbone based fluorescent chemo-sensor for the detection of tannic acid and removal of eosin yellow dye
coredata.dc:creator: Priya,
Sharma, Amit Kumar
Kaith, Balbir Singh
Chandel, Kajal
Vipula,
Isha,
Singh, Anoop
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we developed a green strategy for the fabrication of bio-based gelatin/dextrin semi-interpenetrating network (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) for the detection of tannic acid and removal of eosin yellow dye present in water sample. The synthesis of semi-interpenetrating network (semi-IPN) was done under hot air reaction conditions using acrylamide and N,N′-methylenbisacrylamide monomer-crosslinker system. The maximum swelling response of the synthesized semi-IPN was found to be 597% after optimizing all the process parameters. The nanocomposite was derived through in-situ incursion of carbon quantum dots inside the crosslinked matrix of semi-IPN and it was capable of detecting the presence of tannic acid, selectively in wastewater through fluorescence quenching analysis. Maximum quenching efficiency was observed to be 98%. The synthesized devices were also found to be an effective absorbent for the removal of eosin yellow dye from contaminated water. Maximum dye removal potential in case of nanocomposite was found to be 92%. Adsorption isotherm and kinetics studies indicated the applicability of flory huggin's adsorption isotherm along with 2nd order kinetics. The industrial applicability of the samples was depicted from regeneration and reusability experimental essay.

SEM images of (a) hybrid backbone (b) semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) and (c) nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm); (d) TEM image along with the (e) histogram showing particle size distribution; (f) XRD and (g) FTIR spectrum of (i) gelatin, (ii) dextrin, (iii) hybrid backbone (Gel-dex), (iv) semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) and (v) the nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm).

EDS analysis of (a) hybrid backbone (Gel-dex) (b) semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) and (c) nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) along with the (d) EDS elemental mapping of the nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm).

Fluorescence emission spectra of nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) in (a) different organic compounds; impact of tannic acid concentration on (b) fluorescence intensity and (c) percentage quenching efficiency; effect of (d) nanocomposite dose and (e) pH on the fluorosence intensity; (f) Stern-Volmer plot.

Effect of (a) time, (b) initial dye concentration, (c) adsorbent dose, and (d) pH on the percentage dye removal efficiency of the semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm); adsorption Isotherm for the adsorption of eosin yellow onto the semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) (e) Freundlich isotherm (f) Langmuir isotherm (g) Temkin Isotherm, and (h) Flory-Huggins; kinetic models for the adsorption of eosin yellow using (i) First order kinetics, (j) Second order kinetics, (k) Intra-particle diffusion, and (l) Elovich model.

row.entry: Adsorbent for the removal of eosin yellow

head.ce:title: Bifunctional gelatin/dextrin hybrid backbone based fluorescent chemo-sensor for the detection of tannic acid and removal of eosin yellow dye

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Dr B R Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar, 144 011, Punjab, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr B R Ambedkar National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Dr B R Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar, 144 011, Punjab, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we developed a green strategy for the fabrication of bio-based gelatin/dextrin semi-interpenetrating network (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) for the detection of tannic acid and removal of eosin yellow dye present in water sample. The synthesis of semi-interpenetrating network (semi-IPN) was done under hot air reaction conditions using acrylamide and N,N′-methylenbisacrylamide monomer-crosslinker system. The maximum swelling response of the synthesized semi-IPN was found to be 597% after optimizing all the process parameters. The nanocomposite was derived through in-situ incursion of carbon quantum dots inside the crosslinked matrix of semi-IPN and it was capable of detecting the presence of tannic acid, selectively in wastewater through fluorescence quenching analysis. Maximum quenching efficiency was observed to be 98%. The synthesized devices were also found to be an effective absorbent for the removal of eosin yellow dye from contaminated water. Maximum dye removal potential in case of nanocomposite was found to be 92%. Adsorption isotherm and kinetics studies indicated the applicability of flory huggin's adsorption isotherm along with 2nd order kinetics. The industrial applicability of the samples was depicted from regeneration and reusability experimental essay.

ce:section.ce:para: Detection and monitoring of organic pollutants is an important part of environmental protection and conservation program. Tannic acid (C76H52O42), a natural hydrolysable polyphenol is widely used in food, chemical, medicine, and leather industry. In food industry, tannic acid is used as a flavoring agent, clarifying agent, food additive, and food adjunct. It is used in several beverages including beer, wine, coffee and black tea [1]. It is one of the major organic pollutants present in industrial wastewater [2,3]. The spillage of tannic acid into our natural water resources and soil can add adverse impacts on our environment. The interaction of tannic acid with the metals present in environmental water produces tannic acid-metal complexes and some other combination products which can be more toxic. Tannic acid may also change the toxicity of toxicants present in aquatic ecosystem and adds negative impact [4]. Therefore, it is very important to develop highly sensitive method for the trace level detection of tannic acid present in natural water.

Different methods have been reported for the detection of tannic acid such as titration [5], protein precipitation [6], electrochemical detection [7], and spectrophotometry [8]. But inadequate sensitivity/stability of the detector and expensive instruments limit the applicability of such methods for detection at trace level. Our method is based on fluorescence quenching experiment to detect tannic acid using carbon quantum dots nanocomposite hydrogel.

Another major threat to our natural water resources is the discharge of harmful textile dyes. They can affect the living water bodies and can also reach out the groundwater sources affecting other living organisms due to their adequate miscibility with water. After their disposal, they can exist for a longer period of time due to the non- biodegradability of the complex dye structures. Therefore some techniques must be developed for the removal of such pollutants in order to save the life of aquatic species. Recent methods of dye removal include electrochemical separation [9], precipitation [10], membrane filtration [11], ozonization [12], and adsorption [13]. Out of these methods, adsorption is the best technique for dye removal applications due to the recyclability and pH dependency of the separation process.

Superabsorbent materials are composed of water-insoluble, hydrophilic and 3-D crosslinked polymeric chains which are capable of retaining a large volume of water or biological fluid under corporeal conditions [14]. Due to the stimuli responsive properties of hydrogel, they find numerous applications in biomedical, agricultural, engineering, and environmental pollution control fields [15–18]. Biodegradable natural hydrogels are more preferable compared to the synthetic ones [19]. Hydrogel nanocomposites can hold the dye molecules onto the adsorbent surface due to the presence of electrostatic interactions between the dye molecules and the adsorbent surface [20].

Dextrin is a low molecular weight carbohydrate containing glucose units linked via α-(1–4) glycosidic linkage. It can be synthesized by the partial hydrolysis of starch or glycogen which can be achieved with the help of acid, enzyme or a combination of both. Dextrin is used for the preparation of adhesives, food, and paper products [21]. It is a hydrophilic polymer containing a large number of hydroxyl groups that can easily be modified. The biomedical importance of the dextrin is due to its biodegradability and biocompatibility [22]. Gelatin can be derived from the controlled hydrolysis of collagen having an amino acid composition similar to that of collagen. The biological macromolecule gelatin finds many applications in biomedical fields due to many important properties of gelatin like low cost, non-immunogenic, biodegradability, and biocompatibility. The grafting of different polymeric chains onto the natural backbone gelatin is feasible due to the presence of a large number of hydrophilic functional groups [23–25].

In the present paper, a green synthesis route has been derived for the preparation of Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm (semi-IPN) and CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm (nanocomposite) using biodegradable natural backbones gelatin and dextrin. The carbon quantum dots nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) was tested for the detection of tannic acid in water sample through fluorescence quenching experiments. The prepared samples were also examined for the treatment of water contaminated with eosin yellow dye and the adsorption phenomenon was evaluated for the recyclability of the samples.

Dextrin (Dex) and gelatin (gel) were procured from Sigma Aldrich. Acrylamide (AAm), N,N′-methylene bisacrylamide (MBA), ammonium persulfate (APS), Eosin yellow (EY) and tannic acid(TA) were acquired from S D fine chemicals Ltd, India.

1 g gelatin and dextrin (1:1, w/w) was dissolved in 24 mL double distilled water. The reaction mixture was kept over the mechanical stirrer for 15 min and after homogeneous mixing, 0.273 mol L-1 acrylamide was added along with the gentle addition of 1.826 × 10−2 molL−1 N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide. This was followed by the addition of 2.55 × 10−2 molL−1 ammonium persulphate in order to initiate the free radical chain reaction keeping the reaction flask in a hot air oven at 65 °C. After 150 min the flask was taken out and obtained semi-IPN (gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) was washed with double distilled water in order to remove unreacted chemicals and homo-polymer. The sample was dried in hot air oven and stored in a desiccator for further applications.

The thermal decomposition method was applied for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). 5 g of gelatin was heated at 270 °C under inert conditions over a hot plate for 15 min until its color changes to brown. 50 mL double distilled water was added and the obtained solution was filtered using Whatman filter paper. The residue was discarded and the filtrate was stored in a refrigerator. 24 mL of filtrate containing carbon quantum dots was used as a solvent for the synthesis of nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm). Before carrying out the reaction, the filtrate was placed in ultrasonicator for 10 min. Afterwards, the optimized amount of acrylamide: 0.273 mol L-1, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide: 1.826 × 10−2 molL−1 and, ammonium persulphate: 2.55 × 10−2 molL−1 was added to it. After homogeneous mixing, the reaction mixture becomes viscous. The reaction was further carried out in hot air oven at 65 °C for 150 min. The obtained nanocomposite was washed with water and dried at 60 °C.

The examination of different functional groups was done using FTIR spectrophotometer (Agilent technology, Carry 630 model, United States) in the range 400–4000 cm−1. Structural morphology and elemental analysis of different samples were studied using Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM Quanta 200 model, Japan). XRD analysis was done using PAN ANALYTICAL X-beam refractometer. Absorption and fluorescence studies were done using UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Agilent Technology, carry 100, United States) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technology, carry 632, United States), respectively. Internal anatomy and particle size analysis was done through Transmission electron microscopy {Tecnai G2 20 (FEI) S-Twin Transmission Electron Microscope, Japan}. CPA-225D Sartius Analytical Valence, India was used for weighing purpose.

The water absorption capacity of the hydrogel was studied at room temperature at neutral pH. 1.0 g dry sample of hydrogel was immersed in 40 mL double distilled water and the sample was weighed after a definite time interval in order to calculate the swelling response. The swelling percentage was determined as per the standard Eq. (1).
(1)
Ps =
Ws – Wd
Wd
× 100
Where Ps = maximum swelling percentage, Wd = initial weight of dried sample and
Ws = final weight of swollen sample [26].

The selective detection of tannic acid in the water samples was done with fluorescence quenching analysis using CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm nanocomposite. For the detection of sensitivity of the nanocomposite, 100 μM aqueous solutions of various organic pollutants such as picric acid, folic acid, uric acid, ascorbic acid, tartaric acid, citric acid and tannic acid were prepared. 0.1 g sample of CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm nanocomposite was placed in 40 mL of each solution and the fluorescence spectra of respective solutions were recorded after definite time interval. The expression for the calculation of quenching efficiency is given by Eq. (2).
(2)
Quenching efficiency
( Q )
=
I o
− I
I o
× 100

Here,
I o
= fluorescence intensity before the addition of analyte and I = fluorescence intensity after the addition of analyte.

The adsorption studies of eosin yellow onto the adsorbent Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm were carried out independently by optimizing the process parameters like contact time, adsorbent dose, pH and initial dye concentration of the solution for the maximum removal of eosin yellow dye. The sorption studies were performed three times for the accuracy in results and residual dye concentration was examined using Carry 631 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer at 517 nm λ max. The extent of dye removal percentage was calculated using Eq. (3) [27].
(3)
% RD =
C i
−
C s
C i
× 100
Where % RD = dye removal percentage, Ci = initial dye concentration (mgL−1) and Cs = solution dye concentration (mgL−1) of un-adsorbed dye.

The dose of adsorbent (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) was optimized by varying its amount from 50 mg to 250 mg in 30 mL of 40 mgL−1 eosin yellow dye solution. Similarly, the impact of initial dye concentration on the dye removal percentage was also determined by varying the concentration from 10 mgL−1 to 50 mgL−1. pH dependent dye removal analysis was carried out in the dye solution of variable pH (3.0–11.0 with 2.0 pH interval) in order to see the impact of pH on the adsorption-desorption process. The adsorption of eosin yellow per unit mass of adsorbent was calculated using equation (4).
(4)
X e
=
C 0
−
C e
W
× V
Where Xe (mg g−1), W (g), V (L) represents adsorption capacity, weight of the adsorbent and volume of dye solution respectively [27].

Ammonium persulphate and water molecules dissociated homolytically under the impact of thermal radiations and produce SO4*- and OH*. These free radicals further propagate the chain reaction and a large number of activated free radical species were produced through the interaction of SO4*- and OH* with the hybrid backbone, acrylamide and N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide molecules. The activated graft copolymer chains of hybrid backbone Gel-dex were crosslinked together with N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide molecules and produces a 3-D structure which further stabilizes the CQDs insides the crosslinked polymer matrix (Fig. 1 ) [28].

Synthesis of semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) was done through the optimization of different process parameters. Under optimized reaction conditions (i.e. reaction temperature = 65 °C, reaction time = 150 min, pH = 7.0, solvent amount = 24 mL, acrylamide = 0.8792 mol L-1, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide = 2.7026 × 10−2 molL−1, and ammonium persulphate = 2.5563 × 10−2 molL−1), the semi-IPN was found to exhibit a maximum swelling percentage of 596%.

The optimum time and temperature were found to be 150 min and 65 °C with maximum swelling percentage of 345% and 355%, respectively. The optimization of reaction time was carried out by varying it from 120 min to 240 min with 30 min time interval and keeping other reaction parameters constant i.e. acrylamide concentration = 0.8793 molL−1, solvent = 16 mL, temperature = 60 °C, pH = 7.0, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide concentration = 2.7026 × 10−2 molL−1, and ammonium persulphate concentration = 2.5563 × 10−2 molL−1 (Fig. 2 a). Likewise, the optimization of reaction temperature was achieved by varying the temperature from 60 °C to 80 °C with 5 °C temperature interval and other reaction parameters were kept constant (acrylamide concentration = 0.8793 mol L-1, solvent = 16 mL, time = 150 min, pH = 7.0, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide concentration = 2.7026 × 10−2 molL−1, and ammonium persulphate concentration = 2.5563 × 10−2 molL−1) (Fig. 2b). It was found that the swelling percentage increases initially up to 345% and 355% with the increase in reaction temperature and time, respectively. After the optimal values of reaction time and reaction temperature, a sudden decrease in the swelling percentage was observed. This trend was due to the generation of sufficient number of OH* and SO4 −* having enough time for their interaction with hybrid backbone (Gel-dex), N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide and acrylamide molecules. After optimum temperature and time, the decrease in swelling percentage was due to the excessive generation of free radicals and formation of compact crosslinked structure [29,30].

The optimal solvent volume for the reaction was found to be 24 mL with maximum swelling percentage (Ps = 481%) under fixed reaction conditions (i.e. acrylamide concentration = 0.8793 mol L-1, reaction temperature = 65 °C, reaction time = 150 min, pH = 7.0, N,N′-methylene bisacrylamide concentration = 2.7026 × 10−2 molL−1, and ammonium persulphate concentration = 2.5563 × 10−2 molL−1) (Fig. 2c). The optimization was done through the variation of solvent volume from 15 mL to 30 mL. It was observed that the swelling percentage initially increases, becomes maximum (Ps = 481%) and then decreases. This trend was due to the formation of more number of OH* which propagate the chain reaction for the formation of crosslinks, but excessive production of OH* can terminate the chain reaction instead of propagating it due to intermolecular collision [31].

The effect of pH was studied after varying the pH of the reaction medium from 3.0 to 11.0 under the constant value of other reaction parameters (acrylamide concentration = 1.318 mol L-1, reaction temperature = 65 °C, reaction time = 150 min, Solvent = 24 mL, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide concentration = 4.0539 × 10−2 molL−1, and ammonium persulphate = 5.4779 × 10−2 molL−1) (Fig. 2d). The highest swelling percentage (Ps = 488%) was found at neutral pH and a decreasing trend in the swelling percentage was observed in either acidic or basic medium. The osmotic pressure theory is well applicable for the swelling response of a weakly charged superabsorbent Eq. (5) [32].
(5) πion = RTΣ(Cig - Cis)
Here πion denotes osmotic swelling pressure, Cig and Cis represent mobile ions concentration in swollen state and in the external solution, respectively.

In neutral medium, the anionic –COO- moieties repel one another and increase the pore size of crosslinks that can hold a large volume of water molecules. In acidic medium, no such repulsions are possible due to the protonation of free –COO- moieties causing structural compactness with less swelling percentage response. On the other hand, highly basic medium favors the screening of –COO- anions with Na+ cations due to charge screening effect and thus decreases anion-anion repulsions with lower value of swelling percentage [33].

The optimal values of acrylamide, ammonium persulphate and N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide were found to be 0.8792 mol L-1 (Ps = 524%), 2.5563 × 10−2 molL−1 (Ps = 502%), and 2.7026 × 10−2 mol L−1 (Ps = 597%).

For the optimization of monomer concentration, the amount of acrylamide was varied from 0.6448 molL−1 to 1.1137 molL−1 under fixed reaction conditions (reaction temperature = 65 °C, reaction time = 150 min, Solvent = 24 mL, pH = 7.0, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide concentration = 4.0539 × 10−2 molL−1 and, ammonium persulphate concentration = 2.5563 × 10−2 molL−1) (Fig. 2e). The amount of initiator i.e. ammonium persulphate was optimized by varying its concentration from 2.191 × 10−2 molL−1 to 4.199 × 10−2 molL−1 under constant reaction conditions (acrylamide = 0.8792 mol L-1, reaction temperature = 70 °C, reaction time = 150 min, Solvent = 24 mL, pH = 7.0, and N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide = 2.7026 × 10−2 molL−1) (Fig. 2f). Similarly, the amount of N,N′-methylene bisacrylamide was optimized by varying its concentration from 1.6210 × 10−2 molL−1 to 3.7837 × 10−2 molL−1 and retaining the constant conditions for all other parameters (acrylamide concentration = 0.8792 mol L-1, reaction temperature = 65 °C, reaction time = 150 min, Solvent = 24 mL, pH = 7.0, and ammonium persulphate = 2.556 × 10−2 molL−1) (Fig. 2g). All the three cases showed that swelling percentage initially increases with the increase in concentration of acrylamide, ammonium persulphate, and N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide, but, beyond optimized value, a decreasing trend in the swelling percentage was observed. The initial increment in the swelling response was due to the increased production of SO4*- and easier approachability of crosslinker-monomer moieties in the neighborhood of hybrid backbone Gel-dex. Beyond the optimum values, the excessive production of SO4*- started terminating the chain reaction producing homopolymer rather than crosslinked product of grafted backbone. Moreover, the increased concentration of acrylamide, ammonium persulphate and N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide enhances the viscosity of reaction medium and hindered the free movement of activated species resulting in decreased swelling percentage [34,35].

The SEM images of hybrid backbone, semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm), and nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) are presented in Fig. 3 a–c. It is evident from Fig. 3a that the smooth surface of the hybrid backbone becomes rough after crosslinking with acrylamide molecules Fig. 3b. The pores and humps clearly indicated the grafting of acrylamide onto the hybrid backbone (Fig. 3c). The nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) possessed a rough morphology with inserted CQDs visible in the nano range (Fig. 1) [36].

TEM analysis (Transmission electron microscopic analysis) was done for determining the size of CQDs nanoparticles. Fig. 3d depicts the TEM image of CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm nanocomposite. Spherically shaped small particles with an average particle size of 2.33 ± 0.02 nm appeared in the TEM image of nanocomposite. The porous matrix of the semi-IPN provided necessary space for the stabilization of CQDs [37]. Fig. 3e represents a histogram indicating particle size distribution.

Fig. 3f represents the XRD patterns of gelatin, dextrin, hybrid backbone, semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) and the nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm). Eq. (6) was used for the calculation of % crystallinity of the samples [31].
(6)
X c
( % )
=
A c
A c + A a
× 100

Here Xc, Ac and Aa represent % crystallinity, area of crystalline region and area of amorphous region, respectively. Percentage crystallinity (% Xc) of dextrin, gelatin, and hybrid backbone (Gel-dex) was found to be 54%, 59%, and 56%, respectively representing the semi-crystalline nature of the materials. The percentage crystallinity of the semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) and nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) was found to be 72% and 81%, respectively. The enhancement in percentage crystallinity value was due to more crystalline and ordered structure of the samples. A cluster of crystalline peaks present in the XRD spectrum of nanocomposite indicated the existence of CQDs inside the crosslinked semi-IPN matrix [38].

The investigations of different functional groups present in gelatin, dextrin, hybrid backbone, semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm), and CQDs nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) was done using FTIR technique (Fig. 3g). The FTIR spectrum of gelatin has shown the presence of a spectral peak at 3292 cm−1 due to symmetric –NH stretching vibration. The peak at 3065 cm−1 was due to –OH stretching vibrations. Other peaks at 2941 cm−1, 1629 cm−1, 1523 cm−1, and 1236 cm−1 corresponds to –CH stretch, -CO stretch of amide, -N-H bending vibrations, and –C-N stretching vibrations, respectively. In case of dextrin, the spectral peaks were found to appear at 3278 cm−1, 2932 cm−1, 1643 cm−1, 1339 cm−1, 1150 cm−1, and 960 cm−1 corresponds to –OH stretching vibrations, –C-H stretching of –CH2, ring stretching of glucopyranose, symmetric deformation of CH2 group, alcoholic –CH2OH stretching mode, and –C-OH twisting vibrations, respectively. The FTIR spectrum of hybrid backbone depicts the presence of all the characteristic peaks of gelatin and dextrin. Compared to hybrid backbone (Gel-dex), the FTIR spectrum of semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) have suggested major FTIR spectral peaks at 3187 cm−1, 2910 cm−1, 1650 cm−1, 1417 cm−1, and 1023 cm−1 due to –N-H stretching of primary amines, -C-H asymmetric stretching, –CO stretching of primary amides, symmetric carboxylate stretching, and -C-H stretching, respectively. In case of CQDs composite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm), certain shifts in the peak position were observed, indicating the insertion of CQDs inside the matrix of semi-IPN [39].

The elemental composition of different samples was analyzed through energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopic studies (EDS) (Fig. 4 a–c). Carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen were found to be present as major constituents. The elemental composition {atomic % (weight %)} of carbon and oxygen in the hybrid backbone (Gel-dex) was found to be 68.16% (57.40%) and 29.65% (33.25%), respectively (Fig. 4a). Upon crosslinking, the elemental composition of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen in the semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) become 52.68% (46.77%), 26.35% (31.17%), and 20.71% (21.44%), respectively (Fig. 4b). The elemental composition of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen in the nanocomposite was found to be 57.63% (50.76%), 25.65% (30.10%), and 15.24% (15.65%), respectively (Fig. 4c). It is clearly evident that the content of carbon increases in case of nanocomposite due to the incursion of CQDs inside the matrix of semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm). The EDS elemental mapping of the nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) is presented in Fig. 4d suggesting the distribution of different elements.

Maximum quenching efficiency was observed with the tannic acid sample indicating the selectivity of the sensor for the detection of tannic acid (Fig. 5 a). The effect of analyte concentration on the fluorescence intensity is given in Fig. 5b. The quenching efficiency with respect to analyte concentration is presented in Fig. 5c. Clearly, the quenching efficiency increases with the increase in analyte concentration.

FRET (forster resonance energy transfer) mechanism well describes the fluorescence quenching of nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) with the addition of tannic acid. The quencher (picric acid) is in the ground state and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) occupied higher energy states. Quenching takes place in a non-radiative pathway through the overlapping of emission spectrum of CQDs with the absorption spectrum of tannic acid. This mechanism is only applicable when the excited state of the quencher and the donor level of the fluorophore are in the range of 10–100 oA apart from one another. Dipole-dipole interactions are the main driving force for the quenching of fluorescence intensity and with the increase in concentration of quencher, the magnitude of driving force increases resulting in enhancement of quenching efficiency (Fig. 5b and c). The effect of fluorophore dose upon the quenching efficiency can be justified in a similar manner [40]. Fluorophore dose was varied from 50 to 200 mg and 98% maximum fluorescence quenching was detected with 200 mg dose (Fig. 5d). The increase in fluorescence quenching with the increase in fluorophore dose (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) can be attributed due to the increased interactions between the fluorophore and the quencher. The impact of pH on fluorescence intensity has been described in Fig. 5e. With the increase in pH, the quenching was found to increase due to the dissociation of tannic acid and formation of a di-anion product.

The mathematical form of Stern-Volmer equation can be given by equation (7) [41].
(7)
F
F O
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
Here, Fo = initial fluorescence intensity, F = final fluorescence intensity, [Q] = solution concentration and KSV = Stern-Volmer constant.

KSV can be calculated from the plot of
F
F O
versus [Q]. Quenching takes place through a dynamic quenching mechanism as indicated by the non-linear Stern-Volmer plot (Fig. 5f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of various dye sorption parameters
ce:section.ce:para: The absorbance of eosin yellow dye onto the adsorbent was enhanced by optimizing various process parameters like contact time, initial dye concentration, adsorbent dose, and pH.

The adsorption of eosin yellow onto semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) was studied for the contact time of 8 h at initial dye concentrations of 40 mgL−1 (Fig. 6 a). Dye removal percentage initially increased with the increase in contact time due to the interactions of dye molecules with the available vacant sites present into the matrix of semi-IPN. After an optimum time interval, all the vacant sites were completely occupied by the dye molecules and saturation was observed around the adsorption equilibrium. The optimal contact time was found to be 6 h with 34.50% dye removal efficiency at neutral pH 7.0 [42].

Fig. 6b represents the % dye removal efficiency of eosin yellow with the variation of initial dye concentration (10–50 mgL-1). 34.50% maximum dye removal percentage was achieved with 40 mgL−1 initial dye concentration of eosin yellow under the optimized time interval of 6 h at pH 7.0. It was observed that dye removal efficiency increases initially with the increase in dye concentration, become maximum and then slightly decreases. Initially, a few numbers of dye molecules were present and the lower % dye removal efficiency was due to the decreased value of driving force towards the available vacant sites present onto the adsorbent surface. But, with the increase in dye concentration, the extent of driving force increases and the dye molecules started occupying the available vacant sites resulting in increased dye removal percentage. After equilibrium, the dye removal percentage decreases due to the non-availability of the vacant sites onto the adsorbent surface [43].

The maximum % dye removal efficiency was achieved by varying the adsorbent dose from 50 mg to 250 mg under pre-optimized contact time (6 h) and initial dye concentrations (40 mgL−1) at neutral pH. The optimized adsorbent dose for the semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) was found to be 150 mg per 30 mL of 40 mgL−1 dye solution, with the removal efficiency of 40.11% (Fig. 6c). The dye removal percentage initially increases with the increase in adsorbent (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) dose due to availability of more number of vacant sites. But, after an optimal dose, there is no appreciable increase in dye removal percentage due to the saturation of the active sites with the available dye molecules [44].

Fig. 6d depicts the impact of pH on the dye removal behavior of semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) with eosin yellow dye. The pH of the dye solution was varied from 3.0 to 11.0. The Anionic dye eosin yellow showed maximum dye removal percentage (81.94%) in an acidic medium at pH 3.0. With the increase in pH of the dye solution, the dye removal efficiency of semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) decreases due to the increase in concentration of hydroxyl ions which competed with the eosin yellow dye molecules for their adsorption onto the positively charged centers located on the semi-IPN matrix. In acidic medium, there is no such competitive adsorption and therefore, maximum percentage dye removal was observed at pH 3.0 [45].

The effect of temperature onto the dye removal behavior of semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) was studied at 25 °C, 35 °C, 45 °C, and 55 °C under the pre optimized reaction conditions of dye removal. The percentage removal efficiency at 25 °C, 35 °C, 45 °C, and 55 °C temperature of adsorption medium was found to be 81.94%, 73.56%, 59.41%, and 52.64%, respectively. The dye removal efficiency of the semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) was found to decrease with the increase in temperature. This was due to the fact that on increasing the temperature of adsorption medium, the magnitude of electrostatic interactions between the semi-IPN and dye molecules decreases resulting in decreased dye removal percentage [45].

Different kinetic models like langmuir, freundlich, temkin, and flory-huggins were studied for the calculation of R2 (non-linear regression coefficient) values and importance of these models to the present study.

Mathematically, the temkin adsorption isotherm can be given by equation (13).
(13) qe = βlogKt+ βlogCe

Kt (Lg−1) and β represents equilibrium binding constant in relation with the maximum binding energy (BM) and amount of heat exchanged during adsorption process, respectively. A plot between qe versus logCe will give the values of Slope β and intercept βlogKt. The values of β represent the binding interactions between the adsorbent-adsorbate molecules. According to Temkin adsorption isotherm, the heat of adsorption decreases linearly due to adsorbent-adsorbate interactions and uniform binding energies of heterogeneous surfaces [47].

This model is used for calculating the extent of adsorption of adsorbate molecules onto the adsorbent surface [47].

The calculated values of R2 were found to be 0.2871, 0.2985, 0.2272, and 0.9395 for langmuir, freundlich, temkin and flory huggin's adsorption isotherm, respectively. The value 0.9395 is closer to unity suggesting the applicability of flory huggin's adsorption isotherm for the adsorption of eosin yellow on to the semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) matrix (Table 1 , Fig. 6e–h).

Fig. 6i-l presents different kinetic models for the removal of eosin yellow through semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm). The calculated values of R2 are presented in Table 2 . R2 = 0.9845 at optimized dye concentration is much closer to unity and indicates that second order kinetic fitted well for the adsorption of eosin yellow [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dye removal efficiency of the nanocomposite CQDs/gel-dex-cl-polyAAm
ce:section.ce:para: Dye removal efficacy of the nanocomposite CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm was studied under the optimized conditions of semi-IPN Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm (i.e. initial dye concentration = 40 mgL−1, contact time = 6 h, adsorbent dose = 150 mg, and pH = 3.0) and maximum swelling response was observed to be 92%. The probable reason for the high dye removal percentage of the nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) was the availability of maximum number of binding sites after the incorporation of CQDs inside the gel matrix.

Regeneration and reusability potential of the semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) and naocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) was studied using standard procedure [49,50]. After adsorption, 1.0 g dye loaded adsorbents were added separately to 1 × 10−1 molL−1 NaOH solution in a 50 mL beaker. Competitive adsorption between the anionic dye eosin yellow and hydroxyl ions (OH−) resulted in desorption of dye molecules from the occupied sites located onto the adsorbent. The samples were taken out and the remaining solution was analyzed with UV–visible spectrophotometer. After this step, the adsorbents (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm and CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) were again placed separately in the dye solutions of optimum concentration. The process was again repeated and the analysis was done up to seven consecutive adsorption-desorption cycles. The nanocomposite showed maximum efficiency up to five cycles and afterwards, the efficiency gradually decreases to 34% (Fig. 7 ). In case of semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm), the maximum dye removal efficiency was found to be observed up to four consecutive cycles and afterwards, the reusability potential suddenly decreases to 33% (Fig. 7). The decrease in the dye removal efficiency was due to the dissociation of the crosslinked network structure of semi-IPN and nanocomposite. The reusability analysis suggested the industrial importance of semi-IPN and nanocomposite for the removal of eosin yellow dye.

Comparative study of the synthesized adsorbents with the already existing adsorbents has proved that they are more effective for the removal of eosin yellow compared to the many other adsorbent (Table 3 ). Also, the novelty to the current research work was added due to the eco-friendliness of the adsorbents as they are derived from natural backbones.

Gelatin-dextrin hybrid backbones were crosslinked through polyacrylamide chains to give semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) and nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) having a unique property for the selective detection of tannic acid and removal of eosin yellow dye. After the optimization of reaction parameters, 597% maximum swelling percentage was found to be observed with the semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm). Nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) displayed 98% maximum quenching efficiency for the selective detection of tannic acid in different organic solvents. The dye removal efficiency of the semi-IPN (Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) and nanocomposite (CQDs/Gel-dex-cl-polyAAm) was found to be 82% and 92%, respectively for the removal of eosin yellow at pH 3.0. Adsorption isotherms and kinetic studies indicated the suitability of flory huggin's adsorption isotherm model along with the applicability of 2nd order kinetics. The regeneration and recyclability essay of the samples explored their industrial importance for the removal of eosin yellow.
